channel.title: Gatsby Starter Blog RSS Feed
channel.description: A starter blog demonstrating what Gatsby can do.

item.title: Unsupervised Domain Adaptation by Backpropagation
item.description: Authors: Yaroslav Ganin, Victor Lempitsky Published In: Proceedings of the 32nd International Conference on Machine Learning - Volume 3…
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yaroslav Ganin, Victor Lempitsky

Published In: Proceedings of the 32nd International Conference on Machine Learning - Volume 37 (ICML’15)

URL: http://proceedings.mlr.press/v37/ganin15.html

Personal Note: easy to understand, quick to implement, an interesting idea

Introduction

In conventional machine learning, we assume that training and testing data are independently and identically distributed. But, in many real-world applications, this is not the case because:

  • Training data are generated
    For example: training an OCR with computer-generated text image samples
  • Target domain data are different
    For example: training a model on IMDB movie reviews to analyze the sentiment of movie tweets on Twitter

Domain Adaptation

Given labeled training data (source domain) and unlabeled testing data (target domain), with the knowledge that they are not identically distributed, we want to train a classifier that works well in the target domain.

Domain Adaptation Neural Network (DANN)

The authors proposed a deep feed-forward architecture that transforms both source and target features into a new feature space. Features from both domains share the same distribution in the new feature space (domain-invariant). The new features can still be used for accurate classification (discriminative).

This architecture has 3 parts:

  • Feature extractor: maps low-level features to high-level features
  • Label predictor: takes features as input and predicts the label in the target domain
  • Domain classifier: takes features as input and predicts its domain (source or target)

Let’s denote the parameters of these 3 parts as θf,θy,θd\theta_f,\theta_y,\theta_dθf​,θy​,θd​ respectively.

The goal of the feature extractor is to trick the domain classifier. It tries to maximize the domain classification loss Ld(θf,θd)L_d(\theta_f,\theta_d)Ld​(θf​,θd​) by extracting domain-independent features. Conversely, the domain classifier itself tries to minimize Ld(θf,θd)L_d(\theta_f,\theta_d)Ld​(θf​,θd​).

At the same time, the feature extractor tries to minimize the label prediction loss Ly(θf,θy)L_y(\theta_f,\theta_y)Ly​(θf​,θy​). We have the objective function:

E(θf,θy,θd)=Ly(θf,θy) − λLd(θf,θd)E\left(\theta_f,\theta_y,\theta_d\right)=L_y\left(\theta_f,\theta_y\right)\ -\ \lambda L_d(\theta_f,\theta_d)E(θf​,θy​,θd​)=Ly​(θf​,θy​) − λLd​(θf​,θd​)

The factor λ\lambdaλ, controls the trade-off between both losses. We are finding parameters θf,θy,θd\theta_f,\theta_y,\theta_dθf​,θy​,θd​ that achieve a saddle point:

(θ^f,θ^y)=arg min⁡E(θf,θy,θd)θ^d=arg max⁡E(θf,θy,θd)\begin{align*} \left(\hat{\theta}_f,{\hat{\theta}}_y\right)&=\argmin E\left(\theta_f,\theta_y,\theta_d\right)\newline \hat{\theta}_d&=\argmax E\left(\theta_f,\theta_y,\theta_d\right) \end{align*}(θ^f​,θ^y​)θ^d​​=argminE(θf​,θy​,θd​)=argmaxE(θf​,θy​,θd​)​

This can be done by stochastic update, where μ\muμ is the learning rate.

θf←θf−μ(∂Ly∂θf−λ∂Ld∂θf)θy←θy−μ∂Ly∂θyθd←θd−μ∂Ld∂θd\begin{align*} \theta_f&\leftarrow\theta_f-\mu\left(\frac{\partial L_y}{\partial \theta_f}-\lambda\frac{\partial L_d}{\partial \theta_f}\right)\newline \theta_y&\leftarrow\theta_y-\mu\frac{\partial L_y}{\partial \theta_y}\newline \theta_d&\leftarrow\theta_d-\mu\frac{\partial L_d}{\partial \theta_d} \end{align*}θf​θy​θd​​←θf​−μ(∂θf​∂Ly​​−λ∂θf​∂Ld​​)←θy​−μ∂θy​∂Ly​​←θd​−μ∂θd​∂Ld​​​

Gradient Reversal Layer

We can reduce the stochastic update into a stochastic gradient descent (SGD) by adding a gradient reversal layer between the feature extractor and the domain classifier. This layer has no parameters. During forward propagation, the output is the same as the input (identity function). During backpropagation, the subsequent gradient is multiplied by \lambda and passed to the preceding layer.

Conclusion

DANN achieves domain adaptation through standard backpropagation. It aligns the distribution of features across two different domains (source and target). It is simple as we only need to add a gradient reversal layer.

References

  1. Yaroslav Ganin and Victor Lempitsky. 2015. Unsupervised domain adaptation by backpropagation. In Proceedings of the 32nd International Conference on International Conference on Machine Learning - Volume 37 (ICML’15). JMLR.org, 1180–1189.
  2. Nicoguaro, CC BY 3.0 https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0, via Wikimedia Commons

item.title: ATP: In-network Aggregation for Multi-tenant Learning
item.description: Authors: ChonLam Lao, Yanfang Le, Kshiteej Mahajan, Yixi Chen, Wenfei Wu, Aditya Akella, Michael Swift Published In: 18th USENIX Symposium…
item.content:encoded:
Authors: ChonLam Lao, Yanfang Le, Kshiteej Mahajan, Yixi Chen, Wenfei Wu, Aditya Akella, Michael Swift

Published In: 18th USENIX Symposium on Networked Systems Design and Implementation (NSDI ‘21)

DOI/ISBN: 978-1-939133-21-2

Personal Note: This is awarded the best paper for NSDI ‘21, highly recommended read

1. Abstract

What do we already know?

To shorten the training time of Deep Neural Networks (DNN) and improve its prediction performance, we usually apply data-parallel distributed training (DT):

  1. Distribute training workloads across multiple workers (processors)
  2. Workers work in parallel during the training process
  3. Training results (gradients) are passed to a parameter servers (PS)
  4. PS(s) combine partial results and return a single aggregated result to the workers

There’a a problem: bandwidth is limited. Better DNN models and faster processors have shifted the bottleneck from computation to communication. Is there a way to speed up the network?

Since we have access to programmable data planes, current trends suggest offloading some of the work to intermediate network devices, instead of passing all of it to the PS(s). This is called in-network aggregation. Related works:

  • NetAgg & CamDoop
  • Parameter Hub
  • DAIET
  • SHARP
  • SwitchML

The authors’ work improves upon SwitchML, so I’ll provide a brief introduction. In SwichML, network switches help aggregate gradients, it solves these problems:

  1. Gradients are large, they cannot be fit into one packet.
    Solution: split gradient into fragments, each packet has a fragment. Use pool-based streaming to aggregate packet by packet.

  2. Switches have limited computation power.
    Solution: switch-host architecture. The switch only does aggregation, the workers coordinate and manage the slots in the switch.

  3. Switches can only do integer arithmetic.
    Solution: workers quantize floating point values to 32-bit integers before sending the packet.

  4. Packet loss and network failure.
    Solution: introduce fault tolerant protocols.

What do we want to solve?

The problem is that SwitchML doesn’t work well for multiple DT jobs. Here are some issues that the authors want to solve:

  1. SwitchML removes the PS and offloads all the gradient aggregation tasks to the switches. When the switch is under heavy usage, workers have to wait for switch resources to free up.
  2. SwitchML performs static partitioning. Each DT job is assigned a permanent partition of switch resources that cannot be shared, which means then when job A is not doing aggregation, other jobs cannot use A’s switch resources. This leads to underutilization.

How did the author(s) solve that problem?

To speed up multiple DT jobs, the authors introduce a network service with the following features:

  • Dynamic: instead of static partitioning, the switch register memory is divided into units called aggregators. Incoming gradient packets are hashed to one of the aggregators, multiple DT jobs now share all the aggregators.
  • Best-effort: when no aggregators are free, the switch forwards gradient packets to the PS.
  • Inter-rack aggregation: when workers and PS(s) are in different datacenter racks, ATP uses a two-level aggregation method explained below.
  • Redesigned network stack: ATP introduces a new reliability algorithm so that in-network aggregation won’t be mislabelled as packet loss. Besides, ATP introduces a new congestion control algorithm to handle multiple DT jobs.

What did the author(s) find?

The authors propose a best-effort, dynamic, decentralised transport layer network service known as Aggregation Transmission Protocol (ATP), which can speed up DT jobs in a multi-job, multi-rack network. The switch handles aggregation and the workers handle reliability and congestion control.

2. Data Structures

Inter-rack Aggregation

Because too many aggregation layers increases routing complexity, and not enough layers reduces performance, ATP only does aggregation at top-of-rack (TOR) switches:

  1. First level, ToR switches at the workers’ rack
  2. Second level, ToR switches at the PS’s rack

Packet Structure

Data is sent over the network with packets, here’s the structure of an ATP packet:

  • IP Header: contains source IP, destination IP, etc.
  • Data: contains a gradient fragment.
  • ATP Header:
    • jobIDAndSequenceNumber: a packet’s ID. Matches gradients from different workers on the same job
    • aggregatorIndex: a hash of <job ID, sequence number> so that the packet maps to a specific aggregator
    • bitmap0: worker’s position
    • bitmap1: first-level switch’s position at the second level switch
    • fanInDegree0: number of workers attached to the first level switch
    • fanInDegree1: number of workers/switches attached to the second level switch
    • edgeSwitchIdentifier: set to 0 when the packet is en-route to the first level switch, and 1 when it is en-route to the second level switch
    • resend: set to 1 if it is a retransmitted packet
    • ECN: set to 1 when network is congested
    • collision: set to 1 when switch is full, and forwards packet to PS

Switch Memory Structure

Switch memory is organised into an array of aggregators, all gradient fragments with the same <job ID, sequence number> is hashed to the same aggregator.

  • bitmap: records which workers’ gradient has been aggregated.
  • counter: records the number of distinct workers included in the aggregated value.
  • ECN: congestion status, set to 1 if an arrived packet has ECN = 1.
  • job ID, sequence number: identifies the current job and fragments serviced by the aggregator.
  • timestamp: updated when an aggregation is performed, when it is not updated for a long time (timeout), the aggregator is deallocated.
  • value: contains aggregated data from all workers of the same job.

3. Network Logic

Switch Logic

Since we’ve gotten the packet and switch memory stucture down, I’ll now explain what happens when an ATP packet leaves a worker and enters a switch. A packet’s ID is its <job ID, sequence number>.

Packet Arrival Stage

  1. A worker computes the aggregatorIndex hash and sends the ATP packet
  2. ATP packet arrives at the switch, switch checks if the aggregator at aggregatorIndex is free
    • If the aggregator is empty:
      • Copy jobIDAndSequenceNumber to job ID, sequence number
      • Go to Packet Aggregation Stage
    • If the aggregator is not empty, but packet’s ID matches the aggregator:
      • Go to Packet Aggregation Stage
    • If the aggregator is not empty, and packet’s ID doesn’t match the aggregator:
      • Hash collision, set packet’s resend = 1, collision = 1
      • Flip packet’s edgeSwitchIdentifier
      • Send packet to PS

Packet Aggregation Stage

  1. Check edgeSwitchIdentifier:
    • If edgeSwitchIdentifier = 0:
      • Store packetBitmap = bitmap0
      • Store packetFanInDegree = fanInDegree0
    • Else if edgeSwitchIdentifier = 1:
      • Store packetBitmap = bitmap1
      • Store packetFanInDegree = fanInDegree1
  2. Flip edgeSwitchIdentifier bit
  3. Compare packetBitmap with aggregator’s bitmap:
    • If packetBitmap is already in bitmap:
      • This is a duplicate packet
      • Worker is resending same packet, network might be congested
    • Else if packetBitmap is not in bitmap:
      • This is a new packet
      • Add packetBitmap into bitmap
      • Aggregate data into value
      • Update counter
      • Update timestamp
      • If counter == packetFanInDegree:
        • All gradient fragments have been aggregated
        • Go to Aggregation Complete Stage
  4. Bit OR aggregator’s ECN with packet’s ECN, store in aggregator’s ECN
  5. Drop packet

Aggregation Complete Stage

  1. The switch sends an ATP packet containing the aggregator’s value and bitmap to the PS
  2. The PS sends a parameter packet to the switch as an acknowledgement
  3. The switch frees up aggregator
  4. The switch multicasts the parameter packet to all workers

You might wonder: “Why don’t the switch just send the result directly to the worker?“. Well, in case the results are lost during transmission, the workers can always ask the PS to resend a copy, and the switch can free up the aggregator to work on the next job.

Reliability Control

Consider this scenario due to hash collision:

  • Aggregator X is occupied.
  • Packets of new job Y, sequence number Z <Y, Z>, which maps to X, arrives.
  • Switch defers the packets to PS.
  • Aggregator X is now free.
  • Subsequent packets of <Y, Z> arrives.
  • Switch aggregates these packets in X.

The switch and the PS each have a partial aggregation of gradient fragments, but they are unaware of this, and won’t exchange missing fragments, and will wait forever. Meanwhile, the workers will think that the long waiting time is due to packet loss, and will resend the same packets over and over again to the switch, but the switch already has these packets.

ATP solves this by not doing in-network aggregation of resent gradients, whether due to actual packet loss or hash collision. This requires coordination at the hosts, first level and second level switches:

  • The hosts retransmit gradient fragment packets with flag resend = 1.
  • At the first level, when the resent packet arrives, the switch checks for a matching aggregator.
    • If a partial aggregation exists, and the resent packet’s fragment has not been aggregated, then the switch aggregates the aggregator’s value with the packet fragment, and forwards the results downstream. The corresponding aggregator is deallocated.
    • Otherwise, the switch forwards the resent packet downstream.
  • At the second level, no aggregation is done, the switch forwards all resent packets and partial aggregated results from the first level to the PS. It also discards all data in the matching aggregator.

Congestion Control

Since multiple DT jobs are sharing the network, when there is a heavy contention of network resources, network congestion occurs. ATP uses a mix of packet loss and ECN flag to detect network congestion:

  • Aggregators keep track of the ECN flag
  • When aggregation completes, ECN is copied into an ATP packet and forwarded to the PS
  • The ECN bit is copied into parameter packets
  • Parameter packets reaches the switch, which are multicasted to the workers
  • Workers see the ECN bit is set, and know that the network is congested
  • The workers, in response, try to reduce congestion by adjusting their sending window in accordance with the Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease (AIMD) policy

4. Conclusion

Overall summary

Although SwitchML supports in-network aggregation for multi-tenant, multi-rack DT jobs, it is very inefficient due to static partitioning. ATP is an improvement over SwitchML as it uses a hash-based dynamic storage, with its own reliability and congestion control algorithms. Quoting the paper:

Testbed experiments show that ATP is able to outperform existing systems by up to 8.7X for a single job, and it is even slightly better than the current state-of-the-art ring all-reduce with RDMA. In a multi-tenant scenario, best-effort in-network aggregation with ATP enables efficient switch resource usage, and outperforms current state-of-the-art static allocation techniques by up to 38% in terms of training time when there is heavy contention for on-switch resources.

Resources

  • Conference website which contains a presentation video, its slides, and the original paper.
  • ATP is implemented as a plugin for BytePS, a DT framework which supports TensorFlow, Keras, PyTorch, and MXNet. The BytePS framework can be found here, and ATP here.

item.title: Face-Spoofing 2D-Detection Based on Moiré Pattern Analysis
item.description: Authors: Diogo C. Garcia, Ricardo L. de Queiroz Published In: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security - April 2015 DOI/ISBN…
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Diogo C. Garcia, Ricardo L. de Queiroz

Published In: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security - April 2015

DOI/ISBN: 10.1109/TIFS.2015.2411394

Personal Note: prerequisites are Fourier analysis, undersampling, thresholding, and some statistics

1. Abstract

What do we already know?

  • Face spoofing detection by combining image-quality analysis, motion analysis, texture analysis.
  • Related work: detect printed and displayed face-spoofing images by using a support vector machine (SVM) to search for a lack of high-frequency information (Zhang et. al.)

What do we want to solve?

  • Video replay attack: attackers show a copy of your face from video calls / recorded videos to bypass authentication.
  • We want a theoretical model that works on any data.

How did the author(s) solve that problem?

  • Face spoofing detection by searching for Moiré patterns.
  • Assumptions:
    • The face image to be checked is on a digital screen.
    • Real images contain most of their energy at the low frequencies in the frequency domain.
    • Face-spoofing images contain unusual peaks at high frequencies in the frequency domain.
  • Modelling:
    • Let N1N_1N1​ and N2N_2N2​ be the pixel lengths of a feature on the face image screen and on the face detection camera.
    • When a camera captures a digital screen, their digital grids will overlap
    • When the sampling frequency of the camera is larger than that of the face image screen, this overlap will cause Moiré patterns to occur (wave interference).
    • Thus, for Moiré patterns to emerge in face-spoofing images: N2/N1>1N_2/N_1>1N2​/N1​>1.
  • Differences from Zhang et. al.:
    • Zhang et. al. assumes that face-spoofing images contain fewer high-frequency content; whereas the authors search for distinct patterns that are high-pass in nature.
    • The author use Difference-of-Gaussian filters to isolate high-frequency patterns in the frequency domain.

What did the author(s) find?

An algorithm that detects face spoofing by finding high frequency peaks other than the baseband. (Baseband signal := information signals at their original frequencies)

2. Contents

Band-pass filtering

Ignore baseband signal so that the algorithm search for peaks at other frequency bands. Convolve the image I\mathbf{I}I with a Difference-of-Gaussians (DoG) filter D(σ,k)\mathbf{D}(\sigma,k)D(σ,k) to get a band-pass filtered image IBP\mathbf{I}_{BP}IBP​.

D(σ,k)=G(0,σ2)−G(0,kσ2)\mathbf{D}(\sigma,k)=\mathbf{G}(0,\sigma^2)-\mathbf{G}(0,k\sigma^2)D(σ,k)=G(0,σ2)−G(0,kσ2)

where G(σ,k)\mathbf{G}(\sigma,k)G(σ,k) is a 2D-Gaussian function. σ\sigmaσ and kkk define the center and width of the frequency band. Repeat the above steps by varying σ\sigmaσ from σ0\sigma_0σ0​ to σmax⁡\sigma_{\max}σmax​ in increments of Δ\DeltaΔ to get multiple IBP\mathbf{I}_{BP}IBP​.

Peak detection

  1. Perform maximum-correlation thresholding: given input image I\mathbf{I}I, we must find a threshold ttt to create a binary image τ(I)\tau(\mathbf{I})τ(I) such that the correlation coefficient ρ\rhoρ between τ(I)\tau(\mathbf{I})τ(I) and I\mathbf{I}I is maximized.
  2. The calculation for correlation coefficient is in the paper, it uses grey levels, frequency of occurrence of grey level, above and below threshold means
  3. After ttt is found, use it to threshold the frequency domain of the band-pass filtered image ∣F{IBP}∣|\mathscr{F}\{\mathbf{\mathbf{I}}_{BP}\}|∣F{IBP​}∣ of size W×LW\times LW×L.
  4. Then, count the percentage of pixels ppp with values above ttt (high frequency peaks).
p=1WL∑u=1W∑v=1Lτ{∣F{IBP}∣}p=\frac{1}{WL}\sum_{u=1}^W\sum_{v=1}^L\tau\{|\mathscr{F}\{\mathbf{\mathbf{I}}_{BP}\}|\}p=WL1​u=1∑W​v=1∑L​τ{∣F{IBP​}∣}
  1. If p<pmin⁡p<p_{\min}p<pmin​ (self defined), repeat step 1 with the next image IBP\mathbf{I}_{BP}IBP​ with increased σ\sigmaσ; Otherwise, it is a face-spoofing image.

Performance evaluation

The algorithm is tested with frontal pose images from the MITCBCL Face Recognition Database, the Extended Yale Face Database B and the Frontal face dataset from the Computational Group at Caltech.

  • Perfect detection for pixel ratio N2/N1≥2N_2/N_1\geq 2N2​/N1​≥2, but for 1<N2/N1<21<N_2/N_1<21<N2​/N1​<2, perfect detection is not guaranteed.
  • False living rate := percentage of spoofing images that are not detected
  • False spoofing rate := percentage of real images that are detected as spoofing images

Results indicate that, in terms of false living rate, the proposed algorithm is outperformed for low pixel ratio values (PR≈1)(PR\approx 1)(PR≈1), but Zhang et. al.’s algorithm cannot guarantee a null false living rate without sacrificing the false spoofing rate.

Zhang et. al.’s algorithm also does not guarantee a null false spoofing rate, as opposed to the proposed algorithm.

3. Conclusion

Overall summary

  • New algorithm for detecting face-spoofing in video replay attacks
  • Face-spoofing image on a digital screen → Moiré-Pattern → peaks of high frequencies detected.
  • Real faces not on a screen → no Moiré-Patterns → mostly low frequencies → pass the check.

Limitations

  • This algorithm only works for faces on a digital screen. Attackers can bypass this by printing your face on a paper / using a 3D mask of your face.
  • Has a maximum distance requirement (see 2.3).
  • Has a minimum resolution requirement for the camera.

item.title: Seam Carving for Content-Aware Image Resizing
item.description: Authors: Shai Avidan, Ariel Shamir Published In: SIGGRAPH ‘07: ACM SIGGRAPH 2007 DOI/ISBN: 10.1145/1275808.1276390 1. Abstract What do we…
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shai Avidan, Ariel Shamir

Published In: SIGGRAPH ‘07: ACM SIGGRAPH 2007

DOI/ISBN: 10.1145/1275808.1276390

1. Abstract

What do we already know?

Traditional image resizing and cropping to support HTML page layouts on multiple screen dimensions.

What do we want to solve?

Resizing images without distorting the structure and content of the image.

How did the author(s) solve that problem?

The authors introduce seam-carving: find paths of minimum ‘importance’ (seams) from top to bottom or left to right of an image, and removes seams to reduce image size or insert seams to extend the image. The ‘importance’ of pixels in paths is defined using an energy function, which allows the algorithm to preserve important details/contents in an image.

What did the author(s) find?

A new algorithm to dynamically resize image by artificially inserting and removing low energy pixels. Usage:

  • Content-aware resizing (e.g. in Adobe Photoshop)
  • Remove objects from images
  • Image retargeting, dynamically resize images for optimal display on different resolutions. However, the produced output is objectively worse compared to many other algorithms (source)

2. Contents

Step 1: Calculate the energy of each pixel

Energy can be calculated using image gradient magnitude, entropy, visual saliency, eye-gaze movement etc. For simplicity, we use gradient magnitude. In this case, we define the energy function as follow:

G(I)=∣δδxI∣+∣δδyI∣G(\mathbf{I})=|\frac{\delta}{\delta x}\mathbf{I}|+|\frac{\delta}{\delta y}\mathbf{I}|G(I)=∣δxδ​I∣+∣δyδ​I∣

where I\mathbf{I}I is the 2D input matrix of an image.

Step 2: Find a list of seams

Next, we want to find seams by ascending order of energy.

What are seams?

A seam s\mathbf{s}s is an 8-connected path of pixels with minimum energy. It goes from top to bottom (vertical seam) or left to right (horizontal seam) in an image.

s={si}i=1n={(x(i),y(i))}_i=1n\mathbf{s}=\{s_i\}_{i=1}^n = \{(x(i),y(i))\}\_{i=1}^ns={si​}i=1n​={(x(i),y(i))}_i=1n

I(si)\mathbf{I}(s_i)I(si​) will then be the iii-th pixel on the path of a seam in image I\mathbf{I}I.

In a vertical seam, each row has one and only one pixel (y(i)=i)(y(i)=i)(y(i)=i); In a horizontal seam, each column has one and only one pixel (x(i)=i)(x(i)=i)(x(i)=i).

Why use seams?

Seams preserve both the rectangular shape and visual coherence of an image.

Objective

Let E(s)E(\mathbf{s})E(s) be the total energy cost of a seam, we want to find an optimal seam s∗\mathbf{s}^*s∗ that minimizes total cost:

s∗=argminsE(s)=argmins∑i=1nG(I(si))\mathbf{s}^* =\text{argmin}_\mathbf{s}E(\mathbf{\mathbf{s}})=\text{argmin}_\mathbf{s}\sum_{i=1}^n G(\mathbf{I}(s_i))s∗=argmins​E(s)=argmins​i=1∑n​G(I(si​))

In practice, this can be done via Dijkstra’s algorithm, dynamic programming, greedy algorithm, graph cuts etc. Here is a dynamic programming example for vertical seams:

M(i,j)=G(i,j)+min⁡(M(i−1,j−1),M(i−1,j),M(i−1,j+1))M(i,j)=G(i,j)+\min(M(i-1,j-1),M(i-1,j),M(i-1,j+1))M(i,j)=G(i,j)+min(M(i−1,j−1),M(i−1,j),M(i−1,j+1))

where MMM is the cumulative minimum energy for all possible connected seams for each pixel (i,j)(i,j)(i,j). Then, we backtrack from min value in last row of MMM to get an optimal seam path.

Step 3: Do something to the seams

To crop the image, remove seams in ascending order of energy as needed; To extend the image, duplicate seams as needed.

3. Conclusion

Limitations

  • May require additional data to reduce errors. For example, images with human faces may require face detectors.
  • If the image is too condensed where every part appears to be equally important, or if an important region is laid out in a way that blocks seams from bypassing, it will be less performant.
  • In some cases, removing a low-energy seam may create a new seam of higher energy. A solution to this is forward energy enhancement. When calculating MMM, the algorithm accounts for changes in energy from new neighbors if a seam is hypothetically removed.

Resources

  • Original paper by Avidan, Shai & Shamir, Ariel. (2007). Seam Carving for Content-Aware Image Resizing. SIGGRAPH. 26. 10.1145/1276377.1276390.
  • Video demonstration on Youtube
  • This website allows you to upload an image and see how it works
  • Many examples of resized images
  • Images generated using a GUI implementation demo by Danny Luong (2021).
